risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Block randomisation of nursing homes will use a computer generated allocation sequence and occur after initial consent. Randomisation will be stratified by organisational affiliation... Randomisation will also be stratified by rurality and dementia-specificity..." Comment: The study explicitly states that allocation used a computer-generated sequence with block randomisation and stratification, which is a proper random method for sequence generation.

Quote: "Block randomisation of nursing homes will use a computer generated allocation sequence and occur after initial consent." Comment: This describes sequence generation and timing, not a concealment mechanism. No explicit central allocation, SNOSE, pharmacy-controlled process, or other method preventing recruiters/enrollers from foreseeing assignment is reported. In this cluster trial, the paper also states managers were not blinded to allocation, but it does not clearly describe how allocation was concealed up to the point of enrolment/participation decisions. Therefore allocation concealment is unclear.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "However, nursing home managers will not be blinded to allocation because they need to make a fully informed decision regarding nursing home participation. Nursing staff and families at nursing homes in the intervention arm will also know that facilitated case conferencing is being implemented because they will be participating." Comment: The paper explicitly states that key personnel and participants/families in the intervention arm were not blinded. The dominant outcomes in this study include family-rated satisfaction with care, symptom-related comfort, nurse-rated comfort/symptom management, proxy quality of life, and staff attitudes/knowledge/confidence, which are subjective and plausibly influenced by knowledge of allocation. Therefore performance bias is at high risk.

Quote: "Research assistants collecting process and outcome data will also be blinded to the specific design and aims of the study, and each assistant will visit nursing homes in either the intervention or usual care arm only." Quote: "Family-rated EOL outcomes will be measured using the End of Life in Dementia (EOLD) Scales... All scales will be rated by families 4 to 6 weeks following resident death..." Quote: "Nurse-rated symptom-related comfort and symptom management will be measured using the CAD-EOLD and SM-EOLD Scales..." Comment: Outcome assessors included patient/family self-report proxies and clinician/nurse proxy raters for subjective outcomes (family satisfaction, symptom-related comfort, symptom management, proxy QoL, staff attitudes/knowledge/confidence). The paper explicitly states families and nursing staff in intervention homes "will also know" the intervention is being implemented, so these assessors were not blinded. Although research assistants were blinded and some outcomes were record-based/objective, the study also includes unblinded subjective/proxy-rated outcomes; by the worst-outcome rule this makes detection bias high risk overall.

Quote: "Given an anticipated dropout rate at the resident level of 10 %, a recruitment sample of 272 residents (17 per site) will be needed for a final sample of 240 residents. We will also over-sample nursing homes by two in each arm (i.e. 10 in each arm, 20 in total) to protect against drop-out by managers." Comment: This article is a study protocol/design paper and reports only anticipated/planned dropout, not observed post-randomization missing outcome data. Although it states that analyses will be on an ITT basis ("Primary and secondary analyses will be performed on an intention to treat (ITT) basis"), it provides no actual per-arm randomized versus analysed numbers, no observed losses at outcome time points, and no reasons for missing data on the outcomes of interest. Therefore attrition bias for the analysed outcomes cannot be judged from reported results and is Unclear rather than High.
